segments.s138-g41: نشستند is a past tense 3rd-person plural verb (they sat). با رودسازان به هم is a prepositional phrase meaning ‘together with the musicians’; the sequence با + noun + به هم places accompaniment after the verb, with به هم (= 'with each other / together') reinforcing the collective action.
segments.s138-g42: بدان تا here expresses purpose/result: بدان (a fused particle/phrase 'to that/so that') + تا ('so that / in order that') introduces a clause of purpose; نباشد is the negative present/subjunctive 'may not be / not be (found)'; دژم is a predicate adjective 'sullen/grim'. The clause therefore gives the reason or aim of sitting together.
segments.s138-g43: چو شد + مست = 'when he became drunk' (چو = when, شد = became, مست = drunk). هنگام خواب آمدش = 'the time/hour of sleep came to him' — هنگام governs خواب ('at the time of sleep'), and آمدش is a clitic-attached past form meaning 'it came to him'.
segments.s138-g44: همی here is an emphatic/adverbial particle (roughly 'suddenly/just/indeed') modifying the ensuing phrase. از نشستن شتاب آمدش literally 'a haste/haste to sitting came to him' — از + verbal noun نشستن marks the source or object of the feeling ('a haste to sit'), شتاب = 'haste', آمدش = 'came upon him'.
segments.s138-g45: سزاوار او جای آرام و خواب = 'a place of calm and sleep appropriate for him': سزاوار is an adjective meaning 'fitting/appropriate', followed by the beneficiary او ('for him'), then the noun phrase جای آرام و خواب ('place of rest and sleep'). Word order is adjective + beneficiary + noun phrase, typical of Persian poetic diction.
segments.s138-g46: بیاراست and بنهاد are past-tense narrative verbs: بیاراست = 'he/they adorned (it)' and بنهاد = 'he/they placed/put (down)'. The verbs list the actions performed (adorned and set out) and are followed by the objects مشک و گلاب ('musk and rosewater').
segments.s138-g33: This clause uses the idiom 'سزا دید' ('he saw it fitting') where سزا functions as an abstract 'worthiness/fit' and دید is the past verb 'saw' meaning 'judged/considered'. رفتن (infinitive) follows to express the action judged appropriate: 'to go'. سوی is the directional preposition 'toward', and خان او places the destination 'to his khan/lord' (خان = title; او = 'him'). Word order is idiomatic: predicate of judgment + infinitive + direction + destination.
segments.s138-g34: شد is the past/resultative 'became' (third person). از here functions as 'on account of / from' introducing the cause (مژده = good news). دلشاد is an adjectival predicate 'glad/cheerful', and مهمان او is the resulting state 'his guest' (literally 'guest of him'). The construction means 'because of the tidings he became glad and was his guest.'
segments.s138-g35: سپهبد is the subject 'the spahbed' (military title). بدو is the dative pronoun 'to him'. داد is the past verb 'gave'. در کاخ جای is the direct object phrase 'a place in the palace' (در + location noun + جای 'place/seat'). Word order S V IO/DO: 'the spahbed gave to him a place in the palace.'
segments.s138-g36: همی + بود is the epic/durative past construction: همی marks continuous or habitual past and بود is 'was'. در پیش او means 'in his presence / before him'. بر به پای is an archaic locative phrase literally 'upon (his) feet', i.e. 'standing on his feet' or 'at attention'. Together: 'he used to stand continually before him on his feet.'
segments.s138-g37: ز ... و ز ... is a poetic repetition of the preposition 'from' (ز). مهانرا is the person called; بخواند here is the past tense verb 'he summoned/called'. Word order places the source phrases before the object: 'from the city and from the army he called Mahānra.'
segments.s138-g38: سزاوار functions as an adjectival nominal 'the deserving/fit one'. با او 'with him' indicates companionship or placement beside the addressee. به شادی uses the preposition به with شادی to mark the occasion 'in/with joy'. نشاند is an archaic past transitive meaning 'sat/placed' (here 'seated'). So: 'He seated the deserving one with him in rejoicing.'
segments.s138-g39: گسارنده is an agentive noun (the one who pours/serves wine); here it functions as the subject. باده is the object 'wine'. آورد is the past verb 'brought'. ساز (placed after the verb) is a second object meaning 'instrument(s), music'. The clause reads: 'The wine‑server brought wine and instruments.'
segments.s138-g40: A string of epithets: سیه چشم is a compound adjective 'black‑eyed / dark‑eyed'; گلرخ is another epithet 'rose‑faced / rosy‑cheeked' (here the adjectival sense, not the proper name). بتان is used metaphorically as 'beloveds/beauties' (idol‑like beauties) and طراز 'in the fashion/style of' qualifies them: 'idol‑styled' or 'idol‑like'. The phrase describes the beautiful women attending.
segments.s138-g25: This is a pair of imperatives/commands. تو (a poetic 'thou') is the vocative/subject pronoun placed before the predicate. مهمان من باش is an imperative clause ('be my guest') with باش (imperative 'be!'). The clauses are joined by و and followed by a negative imperative تندی مکن ('do not be hasty'), where مکن is the classical negative imperative construction.
segments.s138-g26: به کام تو is a prepositional phrase ('to/for your desire') placed before the verb; گردد is a classical present/subjunctive verb here expressing an optative/result ('may it become/turn out'). سراسر سخن (literally 'the whole speech') is the domain affected by the verb. Word order (PP–verb–object) is typical of Persian poetry's compact syntax.
segments.s138-g27: یک امشب ('one tonight') is an emphatic temporal phrase meaning 'for one evening/just tonight'. به می‌شاد combines به + می‌شاد ('would/let (it) rejoice with wine'); می‌شاد is an optative/imperfective verbal form meaning 'be glad' or 'rejoice', and داریم ('we have/keep') takes دل ('heart') as its object: 'we set/let our hearts rejoice with wine.'
segments.s138-g28: وز here is a contracted poetic sequence of و + ز (and + from), introducing a contrast 'and from thought'. اندیشه آزاد داریم دل literally 'we have/keep the heart free (from thought)': آزاد is predicative adjective modifying دل, and داریم is the present 'we have/keep' — together 'we keep our hearts free of thought.' Note: the provided senses list for وز does not include the prepositional 'from', so it is marked below as unavailable.
segments.s138-g29: نماند is the negative past form ('did not remain'); پی here means 'trace/track' and functions as the object. رخش فرخ نهان is a noun phrase 'Rakhsh, (the) fortunate/bright, hidden (steed)' in which فرخ and نهان are adjectives modifying رخش. The whole clause says that no trace of that steed was left.
segments.s138-g30: چنان باره is 'such an occasion/instance' (چنان = 'such'), and نامدار جهان means 'renowned in the world' (نامدار = 'famed', جهان = 'the world'). The clause is tersely stated (poetic ellipsis of a verb) and functions as a result/summary: 'such an occasion made him famous throughout the world.'
segments.s138-g31: تهمتن is the subject (the hero's epithet). به گفتار او is a prepositional phrase 'by/at his speech/words' giving the stimulus; شاد شد is a compound verbal phrase 'became glad' (شاد = 'glad', شد = 'became' — past tense). The clause states a change of state caused by the other's words.
segments.s138-g32: روانش ('his soul/spirit' with the possessive suffix -ش) is the subject; ز is the poetic preposition 'from', so ز اندیشه means 'from thought'; آزاد شد is 'became free' or 'was freed'. Together: 'his spirit was freed from thought.'
segments.s138-g9: درین شهر is the fused prepositional phrase 'در + این' (in this), with ین here representing the demonstrative element; شهر follows it as the noun 'city'. ما is the first‑person pronoun ('we'—a courtly or collective speaker). نیکخواه is a predicate noun 'well‑wisher' and توایم is an archaic/formulaic first‑person present 'I/we am(are) yours / at your service', completing the predicate.
segments.s138-g10: ستاده is a participial adjective 'standing' (i.e., ready). بفرمان is a prepositional/adiunct phrase 'by/at (your) command' (the possessor 'your' is implied). و links the coordinated phrase راه ('way, path, direction') which here means 'your way / your guidance', and the clause closes again with the service formula توایم ('I/we am(are) yours').
segments.s138-g11: تن و خواسته is a coordinated object 'body and wish/desire'. زیر فرمان is a prepositional phrase 'under the command (of...)' with the possessor (you) expressed by the concluding copula. تست here is the archaic/copular form meaning 'are yours' (second‑person), completing the predication.
segments.s138-g12: سر ارجمندان is a nominal phrase 'the heads (or leaders) of the esteemed ones' and و connects it to جان 'life(s)'. آن is a demonstrative/pronominal 'that/their' referring back to what was just spoken, and تست again is the copular 'is/are yours' (poetic second‑person). The whole segment lists valuables submitted to the addressee.
segments.s138-g13: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. رستم is the subject. به گفتار او is a prepositional phrase 'at/to his speech/words' (او = 'his'). بنگرید is the narrative past form 'looked/gazed' (third singular) used to complete the temporal clause.
segments.s138-g14: ز بدها is an ablative/causal phrase 'from/of the bad (things)' modifying گمانیش 'his suspicion(s)'. کوتاه دید is a verb phrase where کوتاه ('short') is predicative and دید ('saw') is the past verb; together they mean 'he shortened/cut short'—i.e., he dismissed his suspicions.
segments.s138-g15: بدو گفت is 'he said to him' (بدو = 'to him'; گفت here introduces reported speech). رخشم is a possessive form 'my رخش' (lit. 'my face/brightness/cheer') acting as the theme of the reported speech. بدین is the fused preposition + demonstrative 'to/at this' (ب + این) and مرغزار is the locative noun 'meadow'—'in/at this meadow'.
segments.s138-g16: ز من دور شد is a clause 'became distant from me' (ز = 'from', من = 'me', دور = 'distant', شد = past 'became'). بی‌لگام و فسار is a descriptive phrase 'without bridle and rein' (i.e., unbridled, uncontrolled), coordinated by و to describe the condition.
segments.s138-g1: چو is the archaic temporal conjunction 'when'. نزدیک شهر سمنگان is an adverbial phrase 'near/close to the city of Samangan' placed before the verb. رسید is a simple past verb 'arrived/reached' and here anchors the clause: 'when (he) reached/approached the city of Samangan.'
segments.s138-g2: خبر is the noun 'news/report' and is the topic/subject. زُو (zū) is the contracted poetic form meaning 'of him/about him' (literally from him). بشاه is the dative construction 'to the king' and بزرگان 'and the nobles'; رسید is the past verb 'reached/arrived' — literally 'news of him reached the king and the nobles.' Word order places the predicate at the end, typical in older/poetic Persian.
segments.s138-g3: که is the complementizer/relative 'that/who' introducing reported information. آمد is the past 'came'. پیاده is an adverb/adjective 'on foot'. گو before a name is a classical/poetic marker meaning 'called/so-called' (an epithet marker); تاج‌بخش is a proper name (literally 'crown-giver') used here as the man's name. The phrase means roughly 'who came on foot, called Taj-bakhsh.'
segments.s138-g4: به+نخچیرگه gives a locative/directional phrase 'to/in the hunting-ground'. ز (here part of زو) functions as a genitive/poetic 'of him' ('his'), so نخچیرگه زو = 'his hunting-ground'. رمیدست is the past verb 'had fled/ran away' and رخش is the name of the steed (subject). The clause therefore says '(for) to his hunting-ground Rakhsh had fled' i.e. 'his steed Rakhsh had fled to the hunting-ground.' Note the verb رمیدست precedes the proper name, a poetic word order.
segments.s138-g5: پذیره شدندش is a verbal cluster meaning 'they received/welcomed him' — شدن with پذیره here yields the past reception and the clitic -ش = 'him'. بزرگان و شاه follows as agent/topic 'the nobles and the king' (word order is fronted predicate then agent, a poetic ordering). In plain prose: 'The king and the nobles received him.'
segments.s138-g6: کسی is 'someone' and کاو functions as the relative 'who' (classical/poetic 'که او' contracted), introducing the relative clause. بسر بر نهادی کلاه corresponds to the expression بر سر نهادن 'to put on (one's) head' (word order altered by poetry): بر/بسر = 'on the head', نهادی = past of 'place/put', and کلاه = 'cap/hat'. Altogether: 'someone who had put a cap on his head.'
segments.s138-g7: بدو is the cliticphrase 'to him' (be + 3rd-person clitic). گفت is the past verb 'said' (here used as main reporting verb). شاه سمنگان is the agent 'the king of Samangan'. چه بود is an interrogative copular phrase 'what/which was (it)' used to ask identity: 'Who/what was it?' The whole front part reads 'The king of Samangan said to him: "What/Who was it..."'.
segments.s138-g8: که is the relative/complementizer 'that/who' linking the question to the following description. یارست is an adjectival/nominative phrase 'was/being a friend/companion' (your companion). با تو is the prepositional phrase 'with you'. نبرد is the noun 'battle' and آزمود is the past verb 'tried/tested' — together 'who, your companion, tried battle with you' (i.e. 'who fought/tried to fight you'). The clause is a relative specifying the identity asked about in چه بود.
segments.s138-g17: کنون (fronted adverb) gives the time 'now'. تا + place phrase (تا سمنگان) here means 'as far as' (limit marker). نشان پی است is a nominal predicate with est ('is') at the end: نشان پی = 'sign/trace of the track' (نشان modifying پی), and است is the copula closing the clause.
segments.s138-g18: وز آنجا is a compact poetic sequence meaning roughly 'and/from there'; آنجا = 'there'. کجا introduces an indefinite location 'somewhere' or 'whither', followed by the nouns جویبار و نی as the subjects/objects and است (copula) at the end: 'there is a stream and a reed.' Word order places the locative and nouns before the final copula.
segments.s138-g19: ترا را is the dative/accusative combination ('to you' with the object marker را). باشد is an optative/subjunctive/correlative use of the verb 'to be' here meaning 'may it be / it will be'. ار = 'if'. بازجویی is the verbal noun 'asking/inquiring', and سپاس is 'thanks' — so the clause reads 'If you ask, thanks will be yours.'
segments.s138-g20: بباشم is first-person subjunctive/optative 'may I be' or 'I will be' used as a promise or offer. بپاداش is a prepositional/adverbial phrase 'as a reward' and نیکی 'goodness/benefit' modifies or is the object of reward; شناس is an adjective/epithet 'knowing/recognizing' (i.e., 'one who recognizes good' or 'grateful'). Together: 'I will be (your) reward — recognize goodness.'
segments.s138-g21: گر is the conditional 'if'. ایدون here functions as an adverbial/temporal element ('to this time / until now' in archaic/poetic usage), followed by که introducing the clause. ماند (present/past sense 'remains/has remained') takes ز من 'from me' and ناپدید 'hidden/vanished' as its complement: 'if until now anything of mine has remained unseen.'
segments.s138-g22: سران را is topicalized with the object marker را ('the heads/leaders' as the affected ones). بسی سر = 'many/much head(s)' or 'many heads'. بباید + برید is a periphrastic modal construction where بباید ('must/ought') combines with برید (here 'be cut' in force — classical/poetic voice) to yield 'must be cut off' or 'many heads must be severed.' Word order places the topical object before the necessity + verb.
segments.s138-g23: بدو (to him) is a dative clitic/adverb placed before the verb; گفت is the past verb 'said' (here used as 'he said to him'). شاه‌ای is a vocative with the -ای ending ('O king' or 'O ruler') and سزاوار مرد is a predicative phrase 'worthy of a man' or 'worthy man' addressed to the addressee — a conventional greeting/epithet in direct speech.
segments.s138-g24: کسی ... کرد is the basic clause 'someone did (this)'; با تو marks the indirect object/beneficiary 'to/with you' and این کار 'this deed/act' is the direct object. نیارد here functions in context as a negation or an evaluative particle indicating that such a thing is not proper/has not been done (classical/poetic usage); the overall sense is 'no one has done this thing to you.' Word order places the negation/evaluation near the verb with the subject and objects surrounding it.

sentences.s138-s21: They sat together with the musicians so that Tahmatan would not be sullen.
sentences.s138-s22: When he became drunk and the hour of sleep came upon him, a sudden urge to sit came upon him.
sentences.s138-s23: They prepared for him a fitting place of rest and sleep, adorned it, and placed musk and rosewater.
sentences.s138-s17: He deemed it fitting to go to the khan; at the tidings he grew glad and became his guest.
sentences.s138-s18: The spahbed gave him a place in the palace; he stood continually before him, on his feet.
sentences.s138-s19: He summoned Mahānra from the city and from the host; he set the worthy one beside him amid rejoicing.
sentences.s138-s20: The wine‑pourer brought wine and instruments; black‑eyed and rosy‑faced, idol‑like beauties (appeared).
sentences.s138-s13: Be my guest and do not be hasty; may the whole speech turn out to your liking.
sentences.s138-s14: Tonight, for one evening, let our hearts rejoice with wine, and let our hearts be free of thought.
sentences.s138-s15: No trace remained of his fortunate, hidden steed; such an occasion made him famed throughout the world.
sentences.s138-s16: Tahmēten was gladdened by his words; his spirit was freed from thought.
sentences.s138-s5: In this city we are your well‑wishers; we stand ready at your command and are at your road (your service).
sentences.s138-s6: Body and desire are under your command; the heads of the nobles and their lives are yours.
sentences.s138-s7: When Rostam looked upon his words, he cut short his evil suspicions.
sentences.s138-s8: He said to him, 'My face/joy in this meadow has gone away from me, unbridled and without rein.'
sentences.s138-s1: When he drew near the city of Samangan, news of him reached the king and the nobles.
sentences.s138-s2: That he came on foot — called Taj-bakhsh — for his steed Rakhsh had fled to the hunting-ground.
sentences.s138-s3: The king and the nobles received him; someone who had put a cap on his head.
sentences.s138-s4: The king of Samangan said to him, 'Who was it who, as your companion, tried to do battle with you?'
sentences.s138-s9: Now the trace of the path reaches as far as Samangan, and from there somewhere there is a stream and a reed (ney).
sentences.s138-s10: If you ask, thanks will be yours; I will be (your) reward — be one who recognizes goodness.
sentences.s138-s11: If up to now anything of mine has remained unseen, many heads must be cut off.
sentences.s138-s12: The king said to him, 'O worthy man, no one has done this deed to you.'
